justification: All 7 rubric criteria met: (1) Request ID gap identified explicitly as must-have #1, (2) API call duration gap identified in observability review and must-have #7, (3) Metric label valid/invalid gap identified in metrics gaps and must-have #3, (4) Latency histogram absence noted in metrics gaps and must-have #2, (5) PII consideration handled correctly — input address logging placed in nice-to-haves as 'redacted input address on failure' rather than blindly recommended, (6) Verdict is NOT READY, (7) Recommendations are specific and actionable with concrete metric names and implementation details.

observations.text: Request ID gap called out prominently in integration assessment and as must-have #1 with specific guidance (accept from caller header or generate UUID)
API call duration gap identified in logging gaps and addressed in must-have #7 (log duration with errors)
Metric label gap (valid/invalid) identified clearly with rationale about detecting upstream data quality shifts
Latency histogram absence noted with specific metric name suggestion (validation_duration_seconds)
PII handled well — input address logging placed in nice-to-haves with 'redacted' qualifier rather than blindly recommended as a must-have
Verdict is clearly NOT READY with well-structured justification
Recommendations are highly specific with concrete metric names, label values, and implementation approaches
Excellent additional findings beyond rubric scope: health check anti-pattern, circuit breaker absence, retry policy, cascading failure risk
The 3am on-call test narrative effectively illustrates why the observability gaps matter
Proper escalation routing to MJ for architecture decisions and Bird for domain decisions
